DISCOVERY 302, PLAINTIFF UNITED BRANDS PRODUCTS DESIGN, DEVELOPMENT AND MARKETING, INC'S MOTION TO COMPEL FURTHER DEPOSITION TESTIMONY OF DEFENDANT'S DESIGNATED WITNESS PATRICK YEH; REQUEST FOR SANCTIONS AGAINST COUNSEL FOR DEFENDANT MOSA INDUSTRIAL CORPORATION. ARGUED AND THE COURT TOOK THE MATTER UNDER SUBMISSION. FURTHER, THE COURT INDICATED THAT SHOULD ANOTHER DISCOVERY DISPUTE ARISE, THE COURT WOULD ALLOW THE PARTIES TO SET A MOTION ON AUGUST 31, 2011 UPON PROPERLY OBTAINING AN ORDER SHORTENING TIME. AFTER HEARING THE COURT RULED AS FOLLOWS: PLAINTIFF'S MOTION TO COMPEL FURTHER DEPOSITION TESTIMONY IS GRANTED AS TO QUESTION 1, PLAINTIFF QUESTIONING YEH AS TO WHAT DOCUMENTS HE REVIEWED TO PREPARE FOR THE DEPOSITION IS PROPER. GRANTED AS TO QUESTIONS 2-4 AND 21-23. THESE QUESTIONS ARE RELEVANT TO ISSUES IN THIS CASE INCLUDING DEFENDANTS' AFFIRMATIVE DEFENSES OF ILLEGALITY AND UNCLEAN HAND. GRANTED AS TO QUESTIONS 8-12 AND 13-20. THE SUBJECT MATTER OF THESE COMMUNICATIONS WAS WAIVED AND PLACED DIRECTLY AT ISSUE BY MR. YEH'S EMAILS. DEFENDANT'S REQUEST FOR RECONSIDERATION IS DENIED. BECAUSE THE WITNESSES HERE ARE DESIGNATED AS THE P.M.K. FOR DEFENDANT, THE TOYOTA MOTOR CORP. CASE IS CLEARLY DISTINGUISHABLE. MOREOVER, DEFENDANT HAS NOT PROPERLY RAISED THIS ISSUE. REQUESTS OF BOTH PARTIES FOR SANCTIONS IS DENIED. THE COURT MODIFIED AND SIGNED PLAINTIFF'S PROPOSED ORDER. COUNSEL NOTIFIED OF THE COURT'S RULING ON AUGUST 15, 2011.

MINI MINUTES:
DEFENDANT/CROSS-COMPLAINANT MOSA INDUSTRIAL CORPORATION'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT, OR, IN THE ALTERNATIVE, FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION ON DEFENDANT'S THIRD AMENDED CROSS-COMPLAINT.

APPEARANCES: Jon R. Parsons, Esq. 650/321-8579 & Heather Ledgerwood, Esq. 650/321-8722 of Jon R. Parsons Law Firm for United Brands Products Design Development and Marketing, Inc.; Robert M. Vantress, Esq. of Vantress Law Group 408/905-6501 for Mosa Industrial Corporation.

RULING: Argued and the Court adopted its tentative ruling - Cross-Plaintiff's MSA/MSJ to the 1st cause of action (Breach of Contract) in the TAXC is denied. Cross-Pl. has argued that it duly performed under the contract by delivering good ordered by Cross-Def. However, as seen from the Court's analyses in the other motions, whether Cross-Plaintiff performed under the agreement is disputed.
Cross-Plaintiff's MSA/MSJ to the 5th - 8th causes of action (Common Counts) in the TAXC is also denied. While Cross Plaintiff has shifted the burden on each of the common counts, Cross Defendant has successfully raised numerous triable issues of fact precluding MSA. For example, Cross-Def. raises triable issues of fact as to whether the price listed and charged on each invoice was accurate and whether at least onE invoice was already paid. Also, there are triable issues of fact as to the amount of damages. (PREVAILING PARTY TO PREPARE ORDER.) Three orders on objections signed. (302/LMG) Judge: Loretta M. Giorgi, Reporter: Karla Ellis-Davis, CSR #12998

Defendant Mosa Industrial Corporation Motion For Summary Judgment
Off Calendar Sep-01-2011 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
DEFENDANT/CROSS-COMPLAINANT MOSA INDUSTRIAL CORPORATION'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT, OR, IN THE ALTERNATIVE, FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION RE LACK OF MERIT OF COMPLAINT.

APPEARANCES: Jon R. Parsons, Esq. 650/321-8579 & Heather Ledgerwood, Esq. 650/321-8722 of Jon R. Parsons Law Firm for United Brands Products Design Development and Marketing, Inc.; Robert M. Vantress, Esq. of Vantress Law Group 408/905-6501 for Mosa Industrial Corporation.

RULING: Argued and the Court adopted its tentative ruling -
Defendant's Motion for Summary Adjudication/ and Summary Judgment as to the breach of contract cause of action in the Second Amended Complaint is denied. While the Agreement is very poorly written, it appears that Plaintiff is correct that the quantity term appears clearly within its four corners - "United shall ship minimum two containers in monthly". The analysis regarding breach/repudiation is the same as set forth in the tentative ruling on Plaintiff's MSA on the same issue.
Defendant's argument that even if Plaintiff could prove repudiation, it did not actually cancel the Agreement is inapposite. Plaintiff dOes not have to prove it canceled the Agreement in order to prevail on its breach of contract claim. Finally, on the alleged breach of the exclusivity provision of the Agreement, Plaintiff has likewise raised a triable issue of fact that Defendant sold product to Brown-Halco in North America through its subsidiary in violation of the Agreement. Plaintiff has not provided any evidence of damage on this claim, but only because Defendant has not yet provided the documents it was compelled to provide. (PREVAILING PARTY TO PREPARE ORDER.) Three orders on objections signed. (302/LMG) Judge: Loretta M. Giorgi, Reporter: Karla Ellis-Davis, CSR #12998

Plaintiff United Brands Products Design, Development And Motion For Summary Adjudication
Off Calendar Sep-01-2011 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
PLAINTIFF/CROSS-DEFENDANT UNITED BRANDS PRODUCTS DESIGN DEVELOPMENT AND MARKETING, INC.'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION AGAINST DEFENDANT AND CROSS-COMPLAINANT MOSA.

APPEARANCES: Jon R. Parsons, Esq. 650/321-8579 & Heather Ledgerwood, Esq. 650/321-8722 of Jon R. Parsons Law Firm for United Brands Products Design Development and Marketing, Inc.; Robert M. Vantress, Esq. of Vantress Law Group 408/905-6501 for Mosa Industrial Corporation.

RULING: Argued and the Court adopted its tentative ruling -
Plaintiff's Motion for Summary Adjudication on the Breach of Contract cause of action is denied. Plaintiff claims that the Court can adjudicate the issue of duty and leave the issue of damages for later. While issues of duty may be adjudicated on a MSA/MSJ, it is limited to the existence and scope of a duty and does not extend to the breach of that duty. Plaintiff asks that Court to determine that Defendant breached a duty that was owed through it repudiation of the contract. This is beyond the permissible scope of a MSA on an issue of duty. Therefore, the MSA is denied.
Plaintiff/Cross Defendant's Motion for Summary Adjudication on the 1st cause of action (Breach of Contract) in the Third Amended Cross-Complaint (TAXC) is denied. Cross Def. argues that Cross Pl. cannot recover for breach of contact because Cross-Pl. repudiated the contract and terminated the agreement. Cross-def. cites to Cross-Pl.'s email to show that Cross Pl. repudiated the contract. However, both parties argue a different interpretation of what the email meant as well as other acts of Cross-Pl. This is enough to raise a triable issue of fact. The Court cannot grant MSA/MSJ based on inferences if contradicted by other inferences or evidence. Aguilar v. Atlantic Richfield Co. (2001) 25 Cal.4th 826, 856.
Cross-Def.'s MSA on the 2nd cause of action (Breach of Implied Covenant) in the TAXC fails for the same reason.
Cross-Def.'s MSA on the 3rd cause of action (fraud/concealment) in the TAXC is granted. This cause of action asserts that Cross-Def. was under a duty to tell Cross-Pl. that Cross-Def. had initiated litigation against Cross-Pl. Cross-Def. argues that it was under no such duty and Cross-Pl. fails to address this issue in its opposition or present any relevant facts.
Cross-Def.'s MSA on the 4th cause of action (Fraud/false promises) in the TAXC is denied. Cross-Pl. has raise sufficient triable issues of fact as to whether it was induced to continue selling product to Cross def. after the agreement was purportedly terminated based on Cross Def-'s promise to promptly pay.
Cross-Def.'s MSA on the 5th - 8th causes of action (common counts) in the TAXC is denied. Cross-Def. argues that the election of remedies rule bars Cross-Pl. from obtaining redundant remedies for one invasion of a primary right. While this may be true, the doctrine does not have any application to the case here. Cross-Pl. is not seeking redundant remedies; it is seeking the same remedy under different theories.
Cross-Def.'s MSA on the 9th cause of action (Violation of the UCL) is denied. Cross-Def. has failed to shift the burden. First, there is no requirement, and Cross-Def. cite to no authority for the assertion that one must be a competitor in order to assert a cause of action under the UCL. Second, there is more that a simple contractual dispute at issue here. Cross-Def. refusing to pay on the invoices is arguably an unfair practice that a jury could reasonably find violates the UCL. (PREVAILING PARTY TO PREPARE ORDER.) Three orders on objections signed. (302/LMG) Judge: Loretta M. Giorgi, Reporter: Karla Ellis-Davis, CSR #12998

After hearing the court ruled as follows: Plaintiff's motion to compel further deposition testimony is granted as to question 1, plaintiff questioning Yeh as to what documents he reviewed to prepare for the deposition is proper. Granted as to questions 2-4 and 21-23. These questions are relevant to issues in this case including defendants' affirmative defenses of illegality and unclean hand.
